data.title: Recession Risk In Autumn 2025 Rising With Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Recession risk has dropped from April according to many economists, including J.P. Morgan and economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal. I am not forecasting a recession now (late July 2025), but the risk rises as tariff uncertainty continues. There are plenty of small negatives which do not add up to a recession. The recession risk comes from the continued back-and-forth from the White House about what tariffs will be.

The economy was well-balanced ending 2024. Inflation had dropped, though still a little above the Federal Reserve’s target. Employment gains were solid, leading the Fed to pause on its interest rate reductions. All looked fairly predictable, and then the second Trump administration changed policies significantly.

On tariffs, we need to distinguish between two effects. One is the tariffs themselves, and the other is continued uncertainty about what tariffs will be. My crystal ball does not show what the president will decide, once and for all, about tariffs. My best estimate is that by the end of summer, all of the major tariff issues will be settled. With that assumption, I do not forecast recession. Let’s roll through that reasoning first, then consider what happens if tariff uncertainty continues through the autumn.

Tariffs will reduce the purchasing power of consumers, which will reduce spending on discretionary purchases. Some layoffs will occur, partially offset by increased production of domestic products. However, the economy will not fall into a recession. Most of the economy is not sensitive to our tariffs nor retaliatory tariffs, so business will mostly continue as usual.

Two other worries do not hurt enough to push the economy into recession. Stepped-up immigration enforcement is slowing the expansion of the labor force, and in some cases, reducing current employment. Agriculture and construction are the hardest-hit sectors, with smaller effects in food service and hotels. This will increase business costs, but the magnitude relative to the entire economy will be small.

Medicaid cuts in the One Big Beautiful Bill have made headlines, but the cuts are from projected spending levels, not actual spending. Medicaid spending will increase annually by about three percent, roughly in line with inflation plus population growth.

With employment not suffering much and inflation still above the Fed’s target, interest rate cuts are unlikely with this assumption of tariff resolution by the end of summer. Financial markets and Fed officials expect two more cuts of a quarter point each in 2025. I am doubtful, because the Fed is likely to wait until it sees actual employment drops, which seems unlikely now.

But what if tariffs do not get settled by the end of summer? That’s what would lead the U.S. economy into recession. News reporters have told stories of businesses delaying capital spending decisions because of uncertainty about tariffs. Some companies are also delaying hiring to fill open positions because of tariff uncertainty. And some consumers fear how tariffs will impact their jobs and are delaying discretionary purchases. So far, these are anecdotes that have not shown up in the official statistics. But the risks are real.

If summer turns to fall and businesses still don’t know what U.S. tariffs and foreign tariffs will be, the anecdotes of delayed spending decisions will turn into data. Spending will drop enough that the country will fall into a mild recession. But the economy won’t spiral downward too far, because so much of America’s spending and production is unaffected by tariffs. The magnitude of a tariff-uncertainty recession would be roughly one-half the usual loss of corporate profits and jobs. For comparison, the 2008-09 recession was twice as bad as usual.

With employment falling, the Fed would certainly cut interest rates. The total eventual cuts would be one percentage point for a short-lived recession, or two points if tariff uncertainty continues well into 2026.

Economic analysis cannot help us figure out which of these scenarios will come to pass. Whether we go into recession or not depends on whether the president settles the tariff issue or continues to change his demands and offers.

In this uncertain environment, business leaders should develop plans for both an economy with continued growth and a recession. My gut instinct says the odds are 60-40 in favor of no recession, but anyone can adjust their numbers based on their own reading of what White House strategy will be.

A base-case scenario would have continued increases in spending and production at about the recent pace, but with slower growth of the low-skilled labor force and slightly reduced purchasing power of consumers.

The recession alternative plan should assume less corporate spending on capital goods and lower consumer spending on discretionary purchases. After tariff uncertainty is over - or mostly over - look for a partial rebound. The rebound occurs because both businesses and consumers will spend the money that they had previously held back. But it will be only a partial rebound, because some opportunities will be lost forever.

In either scenario, business challenges and opportunities will come more from specific markets and production opportunities than the overall economy and recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cipher Mining: Take Profit From The Recent Highs (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Behind Cipher Mining's (NASDAQ:CIFR) volatile price history, there is a persistent declining trend in free cash flow and earnings, even though the business operation has expanded multiple times from where it was a few years ago, and the bitcoin price has climbed to a record high. We don't think the current high price is justified and recommend a sell to take profits.

Review

We initiated the coverage of Cipher Mining in March last year in "Cipher Mining: Fast BTC Asset Accumulation Has Started" when its price was at $3.14 with a Hold recommendation. Since then, its price has gone up many times and by the end of last year was as high as $6.7. But it has also fallen back down many times into the $3-4 range. Right now, its price has changed by 137% since our first article was published. It is a highly volatile stock.

Updates

Despite being volatile from quarter to quarter, Cipher Mining's net income and EBITDA usually grow at the same pace as its revenue, but this past quarter, instead of expanding with the growing revenue, they declined substantially. Its net income once again became negative. The management explained that this is due to the higher QoQ depreciation, in which it depreciated the full value of the new machines installed at Odessa.

Cipher is diversifying its revenue streams by developing its data centers for both Bitcoin mining and High-Performance Computing (HPC) hosting. This allows them to utilize their infrastructure for other computing needs, potentially creating more predictable revenue streams and improving overall utilization economics, reducing reliance solely on Bitcoin mining. In order to realize most of its projects, the company has strategically acquired land in regions with access to low-cost and renewable energy, particularly in Texas, which was approved by ERCOT. Its own presentation shows that it grows to acquire about 657 acres of land in 2025, 250 acres additional in 2026, and a total of another 661 acres in 2027. This focus not only aims at reducing operational costs but also aligns with growing ESG concerns. The company is exploring integration of battery storage, demand response, and other grid-stabilizing technologies to further reduce environmental impact and enhance profitability, potentially attracting more institutional investors looking for "green crypto" solutions.

Expanding mining operations is capital-intensive, requiring significant investment in hardware and infrastructure.To build up a pipeline of projects into 2027, Cipher has been spending $480 million in Q4 2024 and $477 million in Q1 this year totally for property and equipment.

While Cipher aims for disciplined capital allocation, rapid expansion could still lead to debt accumulation or equity dilution if the company needs to raise more capital. Their Q1 2025 GAAP net loss of $39 million, despite adjusted earnings, indicates significant non-cash expenses, which are common in capital-intensive industries. Cipher might really need more investment funds because in the latest quarter, its free cash flow hasn't been this much in the red since it went public. To us, this signals its CapEx spending has reached a near-term limit. Besides the announced and invested projects, it is unlikely to be able to make anymore substantial expansion on its mining operations. Not only is it costly to start a new operation, but the maintenance cost will also be high for all the existing and newly built mining centers. Even those that are committed but not fully paid for will continue to pose pressure on its free cash flow.

Without a sustained improvement in its cash flow, it will either have to resort to borrowing, issuing additional shares, raising private investment funds, or even selling its bitcoin assets. But the increase in total current liabilities shows a significant increase in short-term obligations, which could be a concern if not managed effectively. It could secure more long-term funding through borrowing, but share dilution risks are also real at this point.

While Cipher focuses on low-cost energy, the overall landscape for energy in Texas, particularly for crypto mining, is becoming more scrutinized. Reports suggest that crypto mining has raised electricity costs for Texans and brought the grid "perilously close to failure" in 2023. Increased regulatory pressure or higher energy prices in Texas could negatively impact Cipher's operational costs and expansion plans. The company's reliance on power curtailment strategies, while beneficial for avoiding penalties, could also suggest challenges in consistently meeting production targets if grid stability or energy costs become more unpredictable. By May this year, Cipher is at 9th place on the list ranked by production at 13 EH/s, while most of the competitors ahead of it are at 16 plus EH/s. This is right after the 4th bitcoin halving in April this year. Bitcoin Halving reduces the block reward for miners, making it more challenging for less efficient or smaller players to remain profitable.

Cipher shows resilience post-halving, where it is ahead in production than HIVE Digital Technologies, which has a higher production efficiency than it does, and much more than DMG Blockchain Solutions. But overall, miners with higher production efficiency produced significantly more than Cipher. This highlights the need for Cipher to expand, even facing negative free cash flow constraints, getting back to the point we made earlier. But the constant demand for upgrading the rigs and machines from the intense competition and halving impact has a real impact on its bottom line, as we first alluded to the impact on its earnings at the beginning of the article.

Financial Overview & Valuation

Cipher Mining valuation is significantly higher if only compared with the information technology/application software sector without the bitcoin component. Added with Bitcoin, the company is valued almost 8x of the average of these peers, but is still lower than another fellow bitcoin miner, Hut 8 (HUT). In this sense, it seems to be fairly valued.

But when comparing with net income margin on a TTM basis, it stood out at the bottom at negative 81.21%, as opposed to the negative 39.92% by Hut 8. The weakening earnings and free cash flow margin is alarming to us, especially when the bitcoin price is climbing one new high after another. We don't think the recent run up from $2.2 in April to now close to $7 isn't justified. We recommend investors who have made profits in their trade to take profits and exit the position.

Conclusion

Cipher Mining presents a compelling growth story driven by its strategic expansion, focus on low-cost energy, and diversification efforts. However, investors must weigh these positives against the inherent volatility of Bitcoin, potential energy market challenges, and intense competition within the mining sector. These aspects are more constant than any potential trend in its stock price. The deep dive of its free cash flow and net income are concerning to us, under the backdrop of record-high bitcoin prices. We recommend a sell at these levels.

data.title: First Financial: Dividend Growth Continues, Over 4% Yield
data.description:
We continue our pivotal Q2 earnings season regional bank coverage with a moderately high-yielding stock in First Financial Bancorp. (NASDAQ:FFBC). As we have noted, we like to review the regional banks' performance, as well as commentary from leadership of the banks to understand strengths or weaknesses in local economies in the U.S. Thus far this earnings season, we have not really seen much in the way of concerns with the economy from the regional banks. Further, most are still growing loans and deposits, while asset quality is holding up. Of course, mileage varies based on the bank, the region, and the types of loans they prefer (think commercial versus consumer). Some banks have expanded loan loss provisions out of caution, but very few seem like they are concerned with non-payment. Let us discuss the performance in Q2 of this Ohio-based bank. We think FFBC is a good choice for income, and the company just raised its dividend as it reported Q2 earnings.

First Financial Bancorp Q2 Earnings: A Double-Line Beat

First Financial saw a 5.6% increase in top-line revenues to $226.8 million from the previous year's Q2, and this surpassed consensus estimates by $6.9 million. Net interest income remains strong, and we saw a nice expansion in this metric from the sequential quarter. It was up 17 basis points, hitting a strong 4.05% on a fully tax equivalent basis. This is among the higher-end regional banks we cover. It is quite strong. What drove this margin expansion? Margin expansion benefited from the cost of funds which came down 12 basis points, while yields on assets improved 5 basis points. With margin expansion, the bank's net interest income for the quarter hit $158.3 million, up from $153.1 million last year. Factoring in well-managed expenses, we saw an earnings beat here too. Net income hit $70 million, or $0.70 per share, up from $0.54 last year. How about loans and deposits?

Loan and deposit growth

With every bank we cover we look at loan and deposit trends. First Financial saw modest positive growth in loans compared to the sequential quarter. Total loans were $11.79 billion, up from $11.72 billion in Q1. This was 2% on an annualized basis. The bank is forecasting up to 5% loan growth in 2025. This growth was driven by commercial and industrial loans, as well as consumer loans. This helped drive non-interest income higher as well. As for deposits, they increased by $114 million to $14.35 billion, versus $14.24 billion in Q1. These are positive trends for both metrics.

Return metrics improved sequentially

We saw improving return metrics from a rather soft Q1. The return on average assets jumped to 1.52% from 1.13% in Q1. This is also up from a year ago when it was 1.33% and the highest it has been in a year. The return on average equity spiked to 11.16% from 8.46% in Q1, and is up from 9.83% last year. This is also the highest it has been all year. Loan loss provisions were up slightly, however, from $8.7 million, hitting $9.1 million, driven by net charge-offs. The efficiency ratio improved mightily to 56.4% as adjusted, a near 400 basis point sequential improvement. We like what we are seeing here. How about some key asset quality measures we follow?

Well, there was some mixed performance here. Nonperforming assets were up 9 basis points from 0.32% of all assets in Q1 to 0.41% of all assets. These totaled $77.1 million, jumping from $59.8 million in the sequential quarter. The allowance for credit losses held firm at 1.34% of all loans, a single basis point tick up. We take little issue with this change. Additionally, net charge-offs were down 15 basis points to 0.21% of loans, better than the 0.36% in Q1.

We think it is also notable to take a look at the tangible book value. We also saw an improvement in tangible book value per share. Tangible book value per share increased to $15.40, a 4% jump from $14.80. It is also up some 20% from a year ago. This is strong. It is worth noting that tangible book value per share has increased by over 35% in the last three years. Shares are trading at a premium, however, at $25 per share. However, given the performance and the moderately high-yield, and growing dividend, we think a premium is justified.

Final thoughts

With over a 4% dividend yield, along with ongoing projections for 5% loan growth, very strong asset quality, and increasing tangible book value, we like shares of this stock for income and growth. Investors should consider buying in $1.50 drops from present levels on broader market weakness to build a position.

data.title: Intel Q2: Fragile Foundry Ambitions Collide With A Strategic About-Face
data.description:
It’s evident that execution risks remain deeply embedded across both Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) Products and Foundry coming out of the company’s latest Q2 earnings update. Despite outperforming Q2 growth expectations, the company’s margins remained compressed due to incremental restructuring spend. More importantly, management’s commentary on the company’s near-term earnings outlook also conveys a sense of uncertainty, with anticipated spending cuts across both Intel Products and Foundry potentially being overshadowed by looming tariff uncertainties.

We believe the post-earnings stock pullback is reflective of tempered investor confidence given the tepid fundamental outlook ahead of the company’s revised turnaround strategy under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership. Although the Client Computing Group (“CCG”) has maintained growth reacceleration in Q2, it remains insufficient in reversing broader trends of deceleration across Intel. This risks being further exacerbated by ongoing tariff uncertainties leading up to their implementation in August, which has prompted OEM demand for client processors to stay cautious.

Meanwhile, Intel continues to be positioned on the back foot in market’s shift towards accelerated computing due to limited traction for its data center GPUs. Its latest ambitions to build a “full-stack AI solution” is also likely to take years to materialize, while key rivals like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) forge ahead with robust share gains.

The setup accordingly leaves Foundry as a critical pillar for the company’s turnaround. Yet the stark about-face in the unit’s strategy ahead, which includes project cancellations along with a measured expansion roadmap given the tempered demand outlook, dials up uncertainty. This is further exacerbated by limited clarity on whether it’s decided to pursue a fast-tracked roadmap to 14A for external volumes, as well as a lack of updates on chipmaker uptake.

The bleak set-up is also compounded by intensifying competition from key rival TSMC (TSM), whose existing and upcoming advanced U.S. fabs in Arizona are quickly ramping with strong customer pipelines. With escalating execution risks that face no quick and easy fix, we believe Intel’s prospects have only further skewed to the downside coming out of the latest earnings update.

Limited Accretion from Intel Products

Specifically, Intel Products’ strongest segment – CCG – only grew revenue by a modest 6% y/y in Q2, leading to a protracted streak of tepid performance observed in recent quarters. The results are likely indicative of a broader slowdown in its core PC market demand heading into the second half.

Specifically, global PC shipments in Q2 grew by 8.4% y/y, marking the strongest pace of acceleration since 2022. This was consistent with the pickup in Intel’s CCG growth in recent quarters, in line with management’s earlier commentary that the PC inventory correction cycle has bottomed. Yet looming tariff uncertainties are now placing an overhang on PC OEM purchase decisions.

Given limited clarity on the extent of sectoral and reciprocal tariff implications facing the PC market, OEMs are increasingly taking a “wait-and-see approach” in managing supply chains. This is also corroborated by uncertainties on the consumer spending environment when tariffs take effect and pinch discretionary budgets further, making it difficult for OEMs to gauge end-market demand. Considering CCG remains Intel Products’ key growth and earnings driver, it likely remains prone to the ensuing slowdown heading into the second half of the year.

As discussed in one of our previous coverages on the stock, CCG’s heightened reliance on China revenues also makes Intel prone to tariff-related cost and demand risks. Our earlier concerns are now being shared by industry experts who are now acknowledging the impending demand headwind facing PC markets, as the global OEM manufacturing footprint remains highly concentrated in China and prone to U.S.-levied tariffs.

Yet, the global PC manufacturing footprint remains heavily concentrated in China, posing significant challenges for the industry in mitigating tariff risks in the near term.

Due to uncertainty related to the US tariff policy, many OEMs are currently in a ‘wait-and-see’ mode, and OEMs are adjusting their manufacturing locations as tariff concerns continue to loom. These restrictions and supply chain reallocations are expected to be a hurdle for PC shipments in 2025, the research noted.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

The pullback is already evident in Q2’s modest CCG growth, which reflects OEM’s hesitation in stockpiling client processors critical for PC builds later this year. This is particularly concerning, as it coincides with Intel’s upcoming start of volume shipments for Panther Lake – its latest, highest performing client processor based on Foundry’s very own 18A process node. The newest chip, which markets have been confident in as an impending demand driver for Intel, now risks shipping into a broader cyclical slowdown. This could also lead to potential spillover into Foundry, with weak demand for Panther Lake likely to impact 18A’s utilization and unit economics.

Foundry in Focus

Given risks of limited accretion from Intel Products, Foundry has emerged as an increasingly critical component in the broader company’s turnaround efforts despite its inherently capital intensity and ballooning losses. This is further corroborated by the unit’s positioning as the only U.S.-based foundry capable of advanced chip manufacturing at scale, highlighting its geopolitical significance amid rising trade tensions.

Specifically, Intel’s persistently weak traction in the data center GPU market is evidenced in its role of being essentially a non-entity in related opportunities compared to key merchant chip rivals like Nvidia and AMD, as well as emerging custom ASICs competition from Broadcom (AVGO). Admittedly, management’s recent confirmation of a new strategy that focuses on building an inference- and agentic AI-focused hardware-software full-stack offering is a step in the right direction to help Intel better compete against comparable roadmaps currently employed by Nvidia and AMD. But it’ll likely take years to materialize, exposing the unit to continued risks of market share loss in the AI transformation.

This only increases its reliance on Foundry to ensure future relevance and monetization in the AI transformation. Yet the unit’s latest restructuring measures may present as an about-face that increases Intel’s execution risks ahead. Specifically, management’s confirmed cancellation of previously planned fabs in Germany and Poland, as well as the consolidation of certain “testing and assembly operations in Vietnam and Malaysia” as part of its restructuring plan. This is coupled with earlier reports of scaled-back targets pertaining to its Ohio fab expansion project to better align with Foundry’s currently measured external demand outlook.

However, considering robust demand for AI compute, which reinforces the requirement for incremental capacity in advanced chip manufacturing, Intel’s in a race against time to align its Foundry strategy and product roadmap to ensure resonation with customers’ needs. This urgency has become increasingly complicated by intensifying competition from key rival TSMC’s expanding U.S. capacity and domestic customer pipeline.

Specifically, TSMC’s first 4nm fab in Arizona is already in volume production, with comparable yields to those observed at its scaled fabs in Taiwan. Expanded 4nm capacity with TSMC’s second Arizona fab has already completed construction and is on its way to volume production later this year as well, with robust demand secured from leading U.S. customers like Apple (AAPL). Looking further ahead, TSMC is already in process of introducing more advanced 2nm, A16 and A14 process nodes to Arizona through 2028, with about 30% of their capacity to be located in the U.S. by then. This accordingly puts Intel Foundry’s “made in America” advantage on notice, especially as details on external volume uptake remains limited.

Compounding these pressures is the ongoing uncertainty facing Foundry’s external volume production roadmap. As discussed in our previous coverage on the stock, internal management’s reportedly been mulling the potential cancellation of external volumes on 18A to prioritize 14A’s go-to-market instead. However, the risk lies in timing. By the time 14A is ready for volume production at scale, it risks shipping into a normalized end-market demand environment with heightened competition against TSMC’s expanded domestic capacity. This is further corroborated by TSMC’s latest commentary during its Q2 earnings update, which cites observations of a narrowing supply-demand gap for its most advanced 3nm process node and CoWoS packaging capabilities heading into 2026.

It's evident Foundry faces a narrow landing strip for its execution ahead. Any misstep would not only compromise Foundry’s infant credibility in advanced manufacturing, but also risk derailing the broader turnaround narrative that’s only been recently renewed under Tan’s leadership.

Fundamental Considerations

Taken together, we are maintaining our view that Intel’s near-term valuation outlook remains skewed to the downside, especially as the latest earnings update discloses incremental execution risks ahead of its extended turnaround strategy.

Adjusting our previous downside scenario forecast for Intel’s actual Q2 results and updated forward outlook, we now expect full-year 2025 revenue to decline by 3% y/y to $51.5 billion. The forecast incorporates better-than-expected growth observed in the current quarter, which will likely be partially offset by a weaker set-up heading into 2H25 due to risks of tempered CCG growth ahead of tariff-driven tightening in PC OEM spending intentions. This is expected to exacerbate an already-weak set-up in the Data Center and AI segment, as management only now begins focus on a new full-stack strategy to better penetrate opportunities in the transition to accelerated computing. The growth forecast also assumes a downward-revised growth curve in Intel Foundry from our previous analysis, which reflects the unit’s recently slowed expansion plan to better align with weaker-than-expected near-term external demand.

Meanwhile, Intel’s profit margins are expected to remain pressured in the near-term due to the combination of continued restructuring costs as it presses forward with the recently announced job cuts, and Intel Products and Foundry ramp-up costs. This is expected to be further challenged by the upcoming tariff headwinds, which remains an uncertainty as sectoral duties on imported semiconductor equipment and technologies have yet to be announced.

Price Considerations

Our downside scenario forecast anticipates a price target of $15 per share for INTC stock (previously $16).

The price is computed under a discounted cash flow analysis based on Intel’s downside scenario fundamental projections. Key assumptions include a 9.2% WACC, reflecting Intel’s capital structure and overall risk profile. We’ve also applied a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on projected 2029 EBITDA in projecting Intel’s terminal value. This assumption aligns with the anticipated pace of long-term economic growth across Intel’s core operating markets, and adequately reflects the rate at which its steady-state cash flows – as proxied by 2029 EBITDA – can grow sustainably.

Final Thoughts

We believe the latest update on Foundry’s slowed expansion strategy and Intel Products’ delayed shift to a full-stack AI strategy amidst intensifying market competition has only elevated Intel’s execution risk profile. Although the new turnaround strategy led by Tan could potentially end up being Intel’s lifeline, the results remain the be seen. This is further exacerbated by stiffening external headwinds, including adverse implications to both growth and earnings ahead of the upcoming tariffs. Taken together, we believe the Intel stock’s risk profile remains skewed to the downside, and remains prone to volatility from current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tri Pointe Homes: Stepping Over A Very Low Bar
data.description:
The housing sector continues to be moribund, and I expect this to continue to be the case for the foreseeable future which I recently highlighted in this recent article. That said, home builder Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (NYSE:TPH) posted better than expected Q2 results on Thursday.

After declining by more than a third from its October highs, the stock had started to rebound since early June. However, the stock fell just over 10% yesterday even with its 'beat' on Q2 results. The stock is down some 15% from my last piece on it five months ago. Is the equity in the 'buy zone' yet? An updated analysis follows below.

Nevada based Tri Pointe Homes is focused on the building and selling of single-family attached and detached homes. Approximately a third of the company's communities are in California and Tri Pointe also has a home building presence in the Carolinas, Maryland, Nevada, Texas, Washington and Northern Virginia. Tri Pointes sells home under a half dozen brands. The best known are Maracay, TRI Pointe Homes and Quadrant Homes. The stock currently trades around $31.50 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just under $2.8 billion.

Second Quarter Results:

As noted, Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. posted its Q2 numbers on February 24th. The home builder delivered non-GAAP earnings of 77 cents a share, nine cents a share above the consensus. Homebuilding gross margin adjusted to exclude a $11 million inventory impairment charge was 22.1%. Book value has now grown 12.4% from the same period a year ago.

Revenues fell just over 22% on a year-over-year basis to $879.3 million. Despite the large decline in revenues, that did beat analyst expectations by over $70 million. It should be noted that Tri Pointe stepped over a very low bar. The average sales price of a home in the quarter was $664,000 down slightly from $666,000 in the same quarter a year ago. More importantly, home closings shrank to 1,326 from 1,700 in 2Q2024. Order backlog fell even more precipitously from 2,692 to just 1,520 homes.

Management's previous guidance from Q1 called for between 1100 to 1200 home closings in Q2, but at an average price of between $680,000 to $690,000. Leadership blamed the mix of deliveries that were sold and closed in the quarter for the missed target.

Management provided FY2025 guidance calling for between 4,800 and 5,200 homes delivered at an average sales price between $665,000 and $675,000. Tri Pointe's CEO also had this to say about the current housing environment facing the home builder on the conference call that followed the earnings release:

Continued policy uncertainty, geopolitical tensions have weighed on buyer confidence and several markets are experiencing rising housing inventory levels and a softer pricing environment."

In addition, management guided FY2025 homebuilding gross margin to be in the range of 20.5% to 22%. The company continues to offer significant incentives, mainly mortgage rate buydowns and design studio changes, to move inventory. It was not specified in the earnings release or conference call what percentage of sales these incentives were in the quarter. However, incentives at Lennar (LEN) are running around 13% of sales this year, so that probably is a good bogey to model.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Approximately four percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. The company's CEO sold just over $3.6 million worth of equity in late April. However, that has been the only insider activity in the stock in 2025 to date. Since second quarter results were posted yesterday, RBC Capital ($37 price target) has maintained its Hold rating on the stock and Oppenheimer ($38 price target) has reissued Buy ratings on the equity.

Tri Point Homes ended Q2 with some $1.4 billion of liquidity. This consisted of just over $620 million in cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet and $785 million available under an existing credit facility. The company ended the quarter, with a homebuilding Debt-to-Capital Ratio of 21.7% and bought back $100 million of its own stock in the quarter. The company has bought back $175 million of equity so far in FY2025 reducing its float by 5.3%.

Conclusion:

Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. made $4.83 a share in FY2024 on $4.42 billion in revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling to $3.04 a share in FY2025 as sales drop to $3.49 billion. They project earnings to rebound to $3.38 a share on sales growth in the high single digits. Given the uncertainty around the housing sector, I would take any projections for home builder profits in FY2026 with a large grain salt of right now.

That leaves TPH trading at right around 10.5 times forward earnings and 80% of forward revenues. The stock does not pay a dividend. It should be noted that the stock troughed at 40% of sales back in mid-2022 during the last big sell-off in home building stocks as the Federal Reserve embarked on the most aggressive monetary policy since the days of Paul Volcker in March of 2022. That said, Tri Pointe's balance sheet is in good shape.

In summary, Q2 numbers came in over a 'low bar' on Thursday. Given the sell-off that followed yesterday, the market interpreted (and I believe correctly) that things are going to get worse for Tri Pointe before they get better. An investor could reasonably say the same for most home builders right now. Therefore, the best course of action is to avoid a potential 'value trap' in buying home building stocks until the housing environment gets at least marginally more positive. Until then, TPH and most home building stock merit an Avoid rating.

data.title: Charter Communications: Fear Reaches Extreme Levels (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ:CHTR) plunged over 15% on Friday after the company reported weaker quarterly results, which intensified concerns about cord-cutting and even broadband losses. With this drop, shares have essentially wiped out all the gains they had enjoyed over the past year, with enthusiasm over its pending deal with Cox Communications all but gone. This performance has been extremely disappointing, with results weaker than I expected. I last covered shares in May, rating CHTR a Buy. Since then, the stock has lost a quarter of its value, requiring a re-analysis of the thesis.

In the company’s second quarter, Charter earned $9.18, which missed estimates badly, by $0.48, even as revenue of $13.8 billion was in-line with expectations. Residential revenue fell by 0.4% to $10.7 billion while commercial revenue grew by 0.8% to $1.8 billion as CHTR has made inroads with small business customers. Expenses grew by 0.6% to $8.1 billion, causing modest EBITDA margin compression with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 41.4%. As a result, adjusted EBITDA of $5.7 billion was up by just 0.5% from last year. I expected expenses to be closer to flat, which drove the EPS shortfall.

There is such profound fear around the cable company complex about these businesses facing secular decline, amidst changing consumer behavior and rising competition that even modest misses can cause large moves in shares. Clearly, this is not a growth business, as evidenced by the fact while passings were up 2.5% from last year to 57.5 million, its customer count is down 2% from last year to 31.2 million, pointing to ongoing market share challenges.

My view has been that Charter’s business is a melting ice cube: facing ongoing but manageable subscriber losses, rather than one facing sharp decline. So long as customer losses are less than 2% per year, the company should be able to offset that with pricing actions to maintain stable free cash flow. Indeed, its monthly residential bill is $123, up 1.7% from last year. The question is to what extent this quarter undermines this thesis. Frankly, while there were clear areas of disappointment, I believe the market’s reaction is significantly disproportionate to the reality.

Now, at $6 billion, Internet is the primary driver of revenue, and it is Charter’s most important business line. While consumers are able to cut cable TV access, if anything, the need for internet is never greater than now. The pool of internet users should continue to rise, though cable companies’ share of that pool faces pressure from nontraditional competitors.

Internet subscribers fell by 117k to 29.9 million. This was worse than expected and has caused concern about a steepening pace of decline. Internet performance has been increasingly choppy, and the better Q1 result raised hopes we may be seeing a turnaround, which were dashed in Q2. 2024 results were negatively impact by the expiration of government subsidies. For instance, in Q2, net adds were weighed down by the loss of 50k customers as subsidies expired. Excluding this one time impact, internet losses were 18k wore than last year.

Clearly, increased competition from wireless providers and even SpaceX has weighed on cable companies’ broadband results, even as these new technologies have relatively small penetration. Now, despite a 1.5% lower subscriber count, revenue was up 2.8% from last year, given the end of promotional pricing packages and typical bill inflation. Whereas I previously hoped to see internet subscriber losses shrink towards 1% per year, there is increasing risk of 1.5-2% annual pressure. Still, even with this pace of decline, internet revenue should continue to rise.

Now, the decline in video has been a long-term phenomenon. This product is lower margin to begin with given the cost of programming, so declines here have been less painful. There is actually some sign that the pace of losses may be improving, given management actions. Residential video losses were 73k, and video losses were actually the best in over a year. It does appear that the inclusion of several streaming services, like ad-supported Disney+ and Paramount+, has helped to stem the losses of video subscribers. Nonetheless, subscribers are down 5% from last year to 12.6 million. Now unlike internet, where revenue is outpacing subscribers, the opposite is true in video.

Video revenue declines were 9.9% to $3.5 billion. Customers are moving to smaller, lower-cost packages, reducing revenue, however this lost revenue is largely offset by lower programming costs. From an accounting perspective, its streaming package costs of $67 million are also netted against revenue rather than listed as expense. Essentially, these deals are helping CHTR to retain the customer, but it is sharing more of their economics with the streaming service provider.

The one growth engine for the company has been its mobile offering. Mobile growth was 500,000 to 10.9 million lines. Revenue growth remains strong, up 25%, slightly outpacing subscriber growth. However, mobile revenue remains relatively small at $921 million. Still as you can see, mobile growth is steadily decelerating. With over 10 million lines, CHTR has likely converted the majority of its customer base that wants a mobile relationship, which will likely continue to slow mobile growth towards ~15% by the end of next year, still a healthy pace.

Given these dynamics, we continue to see attrition in the share of customers who have 3 products to only having 2 products, largely reflecting customers who drop their cable package, while maintaining a mobile and Internet connection. 58% of customers no longer have a video relationship, and I expect this share to grow, albeit at a slowing rate.

One other headwind aside from its traditional business was that advertising sales were also down 7% to $371 million. Its local news stations benefited from political advertising, which hits its peak in Presidential election years, and we will likely see worsening declines in Q3 and Q4, given this seasonal, unfavorable comparison. Excluding political spending, ad revenue was still down by 4.4% from last year, worse than ~ 2% drop I expected.

Even with this pressure, Charter is a very cash generative business. In Q2, it produced $1 billion of free cash flow. This was down $250 million from last year, primarily due to the timing of tax payments and working capital movements. Cap-ex was essentially flat from last year at $2.9 billion. this spending should moderate over the next three years as it completes its network improvement and buildout project.

With cash flow, CHTR remains aggressive in returning capital to investors. It did $1.7 billion of buybacks during the quarter, or about 1.4% of the share count. There has been 2.2% share count reduction over the past year. I expect the pace of share count reduction to increase as blackouts have passed. With the slower pace of buybacks during blackouts last year, CHTR now has 4.18x debt/EBITDA leverage, down from 4.3x last year. The company carries $94 billion of debt. I expect the company to maintain leverage in this area until the Cox deal closes, likely in 2027, meaning we can expect it to return essentially all free cash flow to investors.

Back in May, I felt CHTR has ~$36 of standalone free cash flow once cap-ex reaches its normalized $9 billion level. Cox should add $2-4 of free cash flow per share. Now assuming a 0.5% worse annual performance in internet net adds, I still see CHTR with $34/share of underlying free cash flow at $9 billion of cap-ex. If the stock stays this low, more share count reduction from the buyback may actually close this $2 gap. That level will be closer to $18 this year, given higher cap-ex spending.

At $311, shares have an ~11% standalone run-rate free cash flow yield, which is really pricing in an ongoing decline in free cash flow. I have previously been using an ~8% free cash flow yield to value CHTR, given my view the company should generate stable to modest free cash flow growth over time. In this environment of fear around the sector, that multiple may be too aggressive, even as I continue to view it as fundamentally reasonable.

Still, I believe CHTR should trade no less than 10x normalized free cash flow. Even with the low-end of Cox synergies, that is $360 and would still support a 10% long-term return even if CHTR cannot raise prices faster than it loses customers. I view this as a conservative assumption, but the market may not be willing to assume much more than this until proven otherwise.

This is dramatically below my $465 prior fair value, reflecting a much lower free cash flow multiple, but I simply struggle to see much multiple expansion given sentiment. Ultimately, while I don't think "fair value" has fallen that much, my price target of what to expect investors will value at CHTR has fallen as fear offsets fundamental cheapness.

Nonetheless, that points to 16% upside under a very conservative assumption, which makes clear just how extreme the market reaction has been. At this point, I believe valuation is becoming very disconnected from fundamentals, and this is a time for patient investors to be buying into the decline. As such, I am moving shares up to a Strong Buy. This selloff is overdone, and with share buybacks ramping up, CHTR will, in my view, recover losses over time and deliver a strong outcome for investors willing to buy into the fear.

data.title: China's Economy Shows Its Resilience Amid Tariff Threats, But Headwinds Remain
data.description:
Recent data show’s China’s economy outperforming expectations. But the world’s second-largest economy continues to face challenges, including deflationary pressures and uneven employment levels. Haining Zha, Vice President and Director, Asset Allocation Research at TD Asset Management, discusses the outlook for the economy and what that could mean for Chinese markets.

Kim Parlee: We are here with Haining Zha, about to delve into what's happening with China.

I want to start with maybe just the highlights. I mean, if you look at the latest metrics coming out of China, I mean, I think the GDP is moving pretty quickly-- the headline. I think, if I look at the markets, I mean, Shanghai is up 9%, Hang Seng has been doing pretty well. Things look really good. Are they?

Haining Zha: I would say there are some pull-forward effect that makes people still worry about the second half of this year. For example, take export. First of all, US and China deal with this trade war really, really well. So it's kind of counterintuitive. If there is one country that dealt with it particularly well, that is China, and they are adversary-- being treated as adversary.

Kim Parlee: I think everyone is being treated as an adversary. But anyway.

Haining Zha: Yeah. And looking at the first half of this year, when you're looking at the, for example, export growth, the overall Chinese export growth is still growing at 7% year-over-year. And the only country that is seeing the decline is-- the only destination seeing decline is basically US, down 10%. All of the other region is growing. One of its biggest partner, ASEAN countries-- as a bloc-- they are growing at 10% year-over-year. Export to Europe growing roughly 4% year-over-year. Even Japan and Korea are seeing some moderate growth.

And also, if you're looking by product, the machinery and electronics, right now, they are already 60% of the overall export. It is growing at 10%. So, within the export sector, it is also having the structural change to move higher to the value chain. So overall, I would say in terms of export, China is dealing it pretty well.

Now, going to the consumption--

Kim Parlee: Yeah, domestic.

Haining Zha: Domestic consumption, last year it was very soft, but this year benefited from the government subsidy and trading program. So far, it has been much better than last year. But the problem is some of the subsidy will be depleted soon, which trigger people's worry about the sustainability in the second half.

Kim Parlee: What about-- I know deflation's on your radar too. And is that something that you're watching in terms of where the economy is going?

Haining Zha: Yeah, deflation is certainly a big topic. So if you look at the China CPI, it is hovering around zero. If you're looking at PPI, it is still negative. So, China is certainly experiencing that deflationary cycle right now.

But on the other hand, that's exactly the reason why the monetary policy has been very supportive. The fiscal policy is incrementally even more supportive. And I think the only thing we worry about is, in terms of monetary policy, people probably shouldn't expect to be-- don't expect huge stimulus, because the rate, the overall interest rate, has already been low. If they further decrease the interest rate, then they basically will hurt the net interest margin of the banking system. And as we see from the European banking experience, that won't be good.

And I think they could later on go onto unconventional paths and have QE and all that. But that really needs systemic shock for that to happen.

Kim Parlee: How is this all going to play out in the markets? I mentioned at the beginning that the Shanghai Composite has almost double digits this year, not quite. Hang Seng has done well. How is this going to translate in terms of Chinese markets and equities going forward?

Haining Zha: Yeah, that's the thing I want to talk about. So, when we hear things about Chinese economy, you have the impression that they are mired in deflationary cycle. So for sure, for the Chinese equity market-- it should be in bear market, right? But what I suspect is we are secretively going into a bull market. It is kind of go under the radar, unnoticed at this moment.

But there are two metrics that I want to mention. Number one is the performance. So if you look at a year-to-date performance, MSCI China or the Hong Kong Hang Seng market is basically generating 20% plus year-to-date return-- outperform all of the other regions. US are roughly 10%. Europe, also 20% in US dollar terms. But if you look in local currency in euro terms, half of that is coming from the currency gain. So if you're looking at local currency, it is also only around 10%. And Japan is basically flat in local currency terms. So it really outperformed.

My second metric is the IPO activity. So this year, Hong Kong IPO market has experienced a revival. So year-to-date, the IPO volume is around 107 billion Hong Kong dollars. So in US dollar terms, that's about $15 billion. So US IPO volume is still higher, sitting around-- the first half of this year-- around $27 billion. But if you look into the mix, about $11-12 billion is coming from SPACs, which is Special Purpose Acquisition Vehicles, not operating companies. So if you remove that, that is actually on par with the Hong Kong market. And we know the US capital market is a magnet of the global capital flow. So for Hong Kong to match that, that's kind of unusual.

Kim Parlee: What's-- I've only got about 45 seconds, so I apologize. What's driving this secret bull market, and will it continue?

Haining Zha: Well, there are multiple drivers. For example, people seeing the AI innovation, the semiconductor breakthrough.

Kim Parlee: It's what's driving the US markets too, at the same time.

Haining Zha: Driving the US market too. But the Chinese people, Chinese market people don't expect innovation to come out of that space. But when they see it, then all of a sudden they want to re-rate that market.

Original Post

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities persevered through tariff and geopolitical scares to deliver solid gains in the second quarter. The broad market S&P 500 Index returned 10.9%, swinging from a near-bear market to all-time high. The benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index did even better as risk-on sentiment took hold, rising 17.8%, and outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index by over 1,400 basis points.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Netflix (NFLX), Eaton (ETN) and Intuit (INTU) as well as underweights to Apple (AAPL) and Eli Lilly (LLY). Stock selection in the communication services and industrials; an underweight to consumer staples.
  • Detractors: Holdings in UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Marsh & McLennan (MMC), as well as an underweight to Broadcom. Stock selection in health care and information technology (IT); an overweight to health care and underweight to IT.
  • Outlook: While the S&P 500 has broadened, the Russell 1000 Growth Index remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through differentiated stock selection.

Performance Review

  • The Fund’s more diversified and defensive exposure compared to our concentrated benchmark reversed from a tailwind in the first quarter to a headwind in the second. We were disappointed with the weak quarterly performance of several of our more countercyclical holdings. UnitedHealth Group saw a renewed selloff following a first-quarter earnings miss and guidance reduction as the managed care company cited elevated cost pressure and the exit of its CEO.
  • Thermo Fisher Scientific lagged due to concerns around spending cuts in the academic/government and biopharma segments due to regulatory fears and tariffs. Insurance broker Marsh & McLennan fell due to the unwind of the flight to quality trade along with concern around a softer insurance pricing environment.
  • Mega cap positioning was mostly neutral as an overweight to Meta (META) and underweight to underperforming Apple offset relative headwinds from underweights to Microsoft and new addition Broadcom (AVGO).
  • Netflix in communication services, one of the Fund’s largest active weights, saw its shares rise due to overall continued robust execution with double-digit revenue growth, driven by a balance of subscriber growth and price, and continued margin expansion. We remain confident in the company’s long-term strategy, strong market position and attractiveness of the global streaming market.
  • Industrials also did well, buoyed by stocks tied to power and electrification, like out-of-benchmark portfolio holding Eaton.
  • In IT, we trimmed Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) to manage the risk amid intensified geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China, directing the proceeds into purchases of AI-indexed chip makers Broadcom and Marvell Technology (MRVL) and software maker ServiceNow (NOW).
  • The additions of commercial airline manufacturer Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and industrials gases provider Linde (LIN) helped diversify the portfolio beyond traditional growth areas and prepare for wider large-cap leadership.
  • Our sales targeted companies whose end markets have become further challenged due to the broader macro backdrop – mass market retailer Target (TGT) and contract research organization ICON (ICLR) — or technological challenges in the AI era – software maker Adobe.

Outlook

  • Boosted by AI enthusiasm along with an easing of volatility and a renewed embrace of risk taking, IT finished the second quarter at 49% of the Russell 1000 Growth Index (RLG), the second-highest concentration since parts of the sector were reclassified in 2018 and 2023. While the S&P 500 Index has shown signs of broadening as judged by the advance/decline line and the number of stocks closing above their 50-day moving average, the RLG remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components.
  • Our longtime IT underweight specifically, and our sector allocations more generally, are a function of managing concentration and industry risk and striving to maintain a portfolio with a diversified set of growth drivers. To this end, the long-term success of the Fund will always be guided by fundamental stock selection. The second quarter is a reminder that there will be periods and market conditions when the Fund will be challenged in keeping pace with the benchmark. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through sector and stock selection investing in differentiated, well-positioned companies.

Benchmark(s)

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Short selling is a speculative strategy. Unlike the possible loss on a security that is purchased, there is no limit on the amount of loss on an appreciating security that is sold short. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. Diversification does not guarantee a pro1t or protect against a loss. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identi1ed or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE. Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

ClearBridge Investments, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

data.title: Brilliant Bargains: 3 Opportunistic Stocks Under $10
data.description:
Trump And The Art Of ‘Perhaps The Largest Deal Ever Made’

Investors breathed a sigh of relief this week as news of multiple 15% trade deals fueled a market rebound and U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent dispelled fears over instability at the helm of the Federal Reserve. As volatility begins to subside, the spotlight is back on interest rates.

On Tuesday, U.S. President Donald Trump announced a “massive” trade deal with Japan. The arrangement would involve placing a 15% reciprocal tariff on the country’s U.S. exports and a reported cut in auto duties, “perhaps the largest Deal ever made.” Separately, the U.S. and the European Union are reportedly close to striking a trade deal that could impose a 15% tariff on European imports.

U.S. equities rallied due to optimism about the trade deal. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq notched record highs on Wednesday, climbing 0.78% and 0.61%, respectively. Gains were led by power equipment maker GE Vernova (GEV), which surged 14.6% an all-time high, and Nvidia (NVDA), which climbed 2.5%. On Thursday, the S&P another 0.3%, while the Nasdaq fell 0.2%.

Bessent put investor anxieties to rest this week that Fed Chair Jerome Powell would need to resign, shortly after Trump acknowledged that he is no longer aiming to fire Powell, stating: “He’s going to be out pretty soon anyway.” Treasury yields inched up higher on Wednesday as a result: the benchmark ten-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was last trading more than four basis points higher at 4.39%, while the five-year yield rose more than five basis points to 3.9% and the two-year yield rose 4.5 basis points to 3.9%. (Find the live chart on Seeking Alpha’s Market Data page.)

Treasury Yields Inch Higher (as of 7/24/25)

Interest Rates Reenter The Spotlight As Volatility Cools

Meanwhile, the CBOE Volatility Index, often called Wall Street’s fear gauge, fell more than 6.8% on Wednesday to a five-month low of 15.37 as market jitters continued to subside.

Wall Street’s ‘Fear Gauge’ Hit Five-Month Low on Wednesday (7/23/25)

The spotlight now turns to interest rates, as volatility cools and Trump doubles down on calls for the U.S. central bank to make cuts. "With a striking of the pen," the president said, the Fed can save the federal government at least $1T in interest rate payments. "Those rates should be three points lower, maybe more than that."

Trump may not get his wish just yet. According to the CME FedWatch tool, trading activity indicates a 97.4% probability that rates will stay unchanged at the Fed's current benchmark policy rate of 4.25%-4.50% at the July meeting.

Fed Futures Traders Are Betting Heavily That Interest Rates Won’t Change

Why Value Stocks Shine In Times Of Market Recovery

As markets rebound, investors often shift from safe-haven assets into more undervalued areas. Value stocks usually perform well during economic recoveries or early-stage bull markets as investors embrace more risk, leading to higher earnings growth and multiple expansion for undervalued companies with strong fundamentals.

At the same time, it is critical to maintain a flexible and resilient portfolio as uncertainty remains over whether the Fed will follow Trump’s wishes and lower interest rates. Striking a balance between value stocks (which could continue to benefit if rates remain unchanged) and growth stocks (which could thrive if rates are slashed) can help prepare your portfolio for whatever comes next.

Using SA’s Quant system, I selected three stocks under $10 to seize upon the current market rebound and growing trade optimism. While I normally avoid low-priced stocks—often the cheapest and some of the most volatile assets in the stock market—the stocks discussed below represent a blend of attractive valuations, above-average growth, and strong balance sheets.

My proprietary method combines powerful computer processing with what I like to call ‘Quantamental’ analysis, designed to remove emotion from the investment process. The SA algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. (Curious how our Quant Factor Grades work? Learn more here.)

In addition to being fundamentally strong, my top three opportunistic stocks under $10 offer high return potential at lower entry points. Let’s dive in.

Seize The Moment: 3 Opportunistic Stocks Under $10

FinVolution Group (NYSE: FINV)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.49B

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 5 out of 690

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 2 out of 38

A member of the PRO Quant Portfolio and one of my Top 10 Stocks For H2 2025, FinVolution Group (FINV) is a consumer lending platform that connects borrowers and businesses across China and other parts of Asia.

FINV is in a strong position following excellent Q1 2025 results. Revenues increased by 9.4% year-over-year, while transaction volume rose 7.9% Y/Y. Its upward climb has benefited from strong growth tailwinds, including China’s push to boost domestic lenders as the government looks to ease regulation in the credit sector.

As SA analyst Bay Area Ideas recently wrote:

FinVolution is showing strong operational metrics, with robust user growth in both China and international markets, indicating rapid adoption and resilience…The stock trades at a deep discount to sector peers, making it undervalued; I am initiating FinVolution at a buy rating based on these strengths.

FINV Stock’s Quant Breakdown

FINV is an incredible bargain, as evidenced by its ‘A+’ valuation grade. Its forward GAAP P/E of 5.95 is nearly a 51% discount to the sector, while its forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 2.68 is over a 75% discount to the 10.83 sector median.

The company also earns an ‘A+’ in momentum, with one-year price performance of 94.7%, an 808.1% difference from the 10.4% sector median. Growth has steadily improved over the past three months from a ‘C+’ to a ‘B’, boosted higher by a forward free cash flow per share growth rate of 61%, a 371% difference from the 13% sector median.

Profitability is also strong, with a return on total assets TTM of 10.65%, an 840.3% difference to the 1.1% sector median. FINV has also received four positive EPS revisions from analysts over the past 90 days, and zero negative EPS revisions.

Next up is another cutting-edge Chinese fintech group offering the perfect balance of value and growth.

LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ: LX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.25B

  • Sector: Finance

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 2 out of 690

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 1 out of 38

Ranked third among all financials and first among consumer finance stocks, LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (LX) is a Chinese consumer finance platform and e-commerce business. The company offers customers personal loans to purchase items on its e-commerce site Fenquile, which carries everything from electronics to clothing to household essentials.

After losing more than 30% during the height of the March/April global sell-off following Trump’s ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs, the Shenzhen-based fintech has bounced back to return 30% YTD thanks to its strong Quant fundamentals and healthy balance sheet.

Highlights from its Q1 2025 results included adjusted net income attributable to ordinary shareholders of RMB472M ($65.7M), a Y/Y increase of more than 100%, and a 19% quarter-over-quarter and 113% year-over-year increase in net income to RMB430M ($60M). The group also announced a share repurchase program this week to buy back “no more than US$50 million worth of its own shares” over the next 12 months

LX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

I predicted LX’s resurgence months ago, but the broader market has yet to catch on to this hidden gem. Despite its strong growth outlook, its share price remains incredibly cheap ($7.55 as of 7/24/25). Fueling its impressive valuation grade is an ‘A+’ forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 4.09, a 63.6% discount from the 11.24 sector median. A forward price-to-sales ratio of 0.64, a 77.5% discount from the sector, emphasizes the low price of its shares relative to its potential sales growth as it continues to expand its lending business.

As SA analyst Chris Bazzle recently observed:

LexinFintech Holdings Ltd.'s pivot to a capital-light, tech-empowerment model reduces risk, boosts margins, and aligns with Chinese regulatory preferences, driving future profitability…Dividend growth, robust balance sheet, and sector-beating momentum reinforce my Buy rating for LX as a compelling deep-value opportunity.

LX earns an ‘A+’ in momentum, with nine-month price performance of 131.9%—a 1,813% difference to the 6.9% sector median. Growth also earns an ‘A+’ with forward EBITDA growth of 34.4%, a 185% difference from the 12% sector median, and forward EPS GAAP growth of 42.3%, a 313.2% difference.

While FINV and LX have made strides in fintech, my next selection is a trailblazer in artificial intelligence.

CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (NASDAQ: COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.78B

  • Sector: Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 11 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 1 out of 39

A top-performing stock in SA’s PRO Quant Portfolio, CommScope (COMM) is leveraging explosive demand for artificial intelligence through its data center infrastructure solutions for hyperscalers, among other tech infrastructure services. The Claremont, North Carolina-based group has returned more than 56% YTD and a stunning 352.7% over the past 12 months.

The group reported strong Q1 results, including a +23% Y/Y increase in revenue to $1.11B (+23.3% Y/Y). Its enterprise fiber business grew 88% Y/Y to $213M, profiting off explosive data center demand this year.

Deutsche Bank recently added the stock to its Catalyst Call Buy list, writing:

We believe business fundamentals remain strong across each of COMM's segments, with recent data points reaffirming this view. Collectively, we believe COMM is likely to trend towards the upper end of its 2025E outlook for Core Adj. EBITDA (DBe: $1.05B, vs. guidance for $1.00-1.05B).

COMM Stock’s Quant Breakdown

I highlighted COMM in May as a top choice for value investors due to its ‘A+’ valuation grade and high growth potential. That assessment still stands: the stock’s forward GAAP P/E of 6.74 is more than a 78% discount from the 31.19 sector median. Its forward non-GAAP PEG of 0.22 is over an 88% discount to the 1.88 sector median.

COMM also scores an A+ in momentum, with three-month price performance of nearly 117%, a 342% difference from the 26.4% sector median. Growth is also impressive, with long-term forward EPS growth of 42.6%, a 198.8% difference from the 14.25 sector median.

With six upward EPS revisions and one negative EPS revision over the past 90 days, analysts appear in line with SA Quant’s Strong Buy rating.

Conclusion: Big Bargains, Big Potential Gains

Like FINV and LX, COMM’s opportunistic balance of value and growth makes it a great option in an improving macroeconomic environment. Maintaining a mixture of both investment styles can help you capture the market's upside while remaining adaptable and resilient should more volatility arise.
